title: GEGLU-Transformer for IMU-to-EMG Estimation with Few-Shot Adaptation
abstract: 神经肌肉激活的可靠估计是实现可穿戴机器人自适应与个性化控制的关键因素。然而，由于电极敏感性、信号非平稳性以及强烈的受试者依赖性，表面肌电图（EMG）在实验室环境之外的稳健部署仍面临挑战。本研究提出一种自适应IMU-to-EMG学习框架，可从可穿戴惯性测量数据中重构异质性运动条件下的连续肌肉激活包络。该框架结合Transformer编码器与高斯误差门控线性单元（GEGLU-Transformer），以增强跨受试者泛化能力并实现快速受试者特异性个性化适配。基于多条件下肢生物力学数据集的严格留一受试者交叉验证（LOSO）协议，该架构在无受试者适配时达到r=0.706±0.139、R²=0.474±0.208的性能；仅使用0.5%适配数据后，性能提升至r=0.761±0.030、R²=0.559±0.047，展现出快速适配与早期性能饱和特性。这些结果支持将注意力机制架构与轻量化适配相结合，作为直接EMG传感技术在真实世界可穿戴机器人应用中的实用且可扩展的替代方案。
html_content:
GEGLU-Transformer for IMU-to-EMG Estimation with Few-Shot Adaptation
1
st
Miroljub Mihailovic,
2
nd
Luca Tonin,
3
rd
Emanuele Menegatti,
4
th
Stefano Tortora
All authors are with the Department of Information Engineering, University of Padua, 35131 Padua, Italy.
Corresponding author: Miroljub Mihailovic (e-mail: miroljub.mihailovic@phd.unipd.it).
Abstract
Reliable estimation of neuromuscular activation is a key enabler for adaptive and personalized control in wearable robotics. However, surface electromyography (EMG) remains difficult to deploy robustly outside laboratory settings due to electrode sensitivity, signal non-stationarity, and strong subject dependence.
In this work, we propose an adaptive IMU-to-EMG learning framework that reconstructs continuous muscle activation envelopes from wearable inertial measurements across heterogeneous movement conditions. The approach combines a Transformer encoder with Gaussian Error Gated Linear Units (GEGLU-Transformer) to enhance cross-subject generalization and enable rapid subject-specific personalization.
Under a strict leave-one-subject-out (LOSO) protocol on a multi-condition lower-limb biomechanics dataset, the proposed architecture achieves r = 0.706 ± 0.139 and R² = 0.474 ± 0.208 without subject-specific adaptation. With only 0.5% adaptation data, performance increases to r = 0.761 ± 0.030 and R² = 0.559 ± 0.047, demonstrating rapid adaptation and early performance saturation.
These results support attention-based architectures combined with lightweight adaptation as a practical and scalable alternative to direct EMG sensing for real-world wearable robotic applications.
I
Introduction
Human–robot interaction (HRI) systems, including upper and lower-limb exoskeletons, powered prostheses, and assistive robots, heavily rely on the estimation of neuromuscular activity to enable natural, safe, and personalised control
[
14
]
. Access to muscle activation patterns enables adaptive assistance, enhances safety, and supports subject-specific control strategies aligned with individual motor capabilities
[
23
]
. However, direct electromyography (EMG) acquisition remains difficult to deploy reliably outside laboratory environments. EMG signals are highly sensitive to electrode placement, motion artifacts, and long-term instability, and exhibit substantial inter- and intra-subject variability in clinical populations, particularly
[
5
]
. These limitations significantly inhibit the scalability and long-term usability of EMG-driven interfaces in wearable robotic systems.
On the other hand, Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs) provide a practical alternative for biomechanical estimation in ecological settings. IMUs enable robust measurement of kinematic and spatiotemporal motion parameters without laboratory infrastructure
[
17
]
. Recent learning-based approaches have demonstrated that inertial signals can be leveraged to infer joint kinetics and even muscle activation patterns from kinematics alone
[
18
,
10
]
. However, most existing methods rely on static models trained offline on multi-subject datasets, implicitly assuming that inter-subject variability can be absorbed during training. In practice, neuromuscular activation strategies remain strongly individualized. As a result, performance often degrades when these predictors are deployed on unseen users, limiting their translational potential.
In contrast, adaptive control is a foundational principle in robotics, routinely employed to compensate for model uncertainty and subject variability
[
28
]
. Controllers update internal parameters to preserve stability and performance under changing conditions. Despite this paradigm, adaptive mechanisms remain largely absent in learning-based neuromuscular estimation, where neural models are typically treated as fixed after offline training
[
13
,
12
]
. Additionally, the validation procedures of these methods often consider simplified scenarios only (e.g., during level-ground walking) without taking into account the variability of real-world environments. This disconnect becomes particularly critical in wearable robotics, where subject-specific neuromuscular dynamics and environmental complexity (e.g., stair negotiation, walking on inclined surfaces), directly affect the effectiveness and safety of assistance
[
25
]
.
In this work, we propose an adaptive learning framework for IMU-based muscle activity estimation. The proposed system combines a neural architecture explicitly designed to enhance cross-subject generalization with a lightweight adaptation mechanism that enables fast personalization to previously unseen users.
The proposed method is validated on a gait-related lower-limb dataset, demonstrating its efficacy across diverse locomotion scenarios (level ground, stairs, slopes, and treadmill walking).
The contributions of this work are threefold. First, we introduce a novel neural architecture for cross-subject neuromuscular estimation from wearable inertial sensing, achieving state-of-the-art performance in level-ground walking while maintaining transferability across users. Second, we systematically evaluate the model across multiple locomotion modes, demonstrating improved robustness to task and environmental variability relevant to real-world robotic deployment. Third, we show that a lightweight subject-specific adaptation requires only a few steps to yield consistent performance gains across conditions, enabling practical personalization within an adaptive robotics framework.
II
Related Work
EMG has been widely investigated as a physiological interface for motion intention recognition and torque estimation in wearable robotics. Recurrent neural networks, particularly Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) architectures, have demonstrated accurate prediction of joint torques and kinematics from EMG signals in both intra and inter-subject settings
[
34
]
. Similarly, deep sequence models have been employed to estimate joint angles and moments from multi-muscle EMG recordings during daily activities
[
30
]
. Beyond purely data-driven approaches, EMG-driven musculoskeletal models have enabled real-time estimation of joint moments and spinal loading during assistive tasks
[
22
]
. Deep learning has also been integrated within exoskeleton control loops, where EMG-based predictions are coupled with impedance controllers to improve synchronization
[
6
]
.
Despite these advances, EMG-based systems remain limited by signal non-stationarity, electrode placement sensitivity, and strong subject dependence
[
15
,
1
]
. Recent benchmarking efforts further highlight limited out-of-distribution generalization in real-world scenarios
[
33
]
. Crucially, most learning-based EMG estimators depend on subject-specific calibration or fine-tuning after offline training
[
19
,
8
]
. While such personalization enhances accuracy, it typically demands quantitative subject-specific data, constraining scalability and imposing significant deployment overhead in real-world wearable robotic systems.
IMUs provide a portable alternative to laboratory motion capture for biomechanical analysis. Convolutional and recurrent architectures have demonstrated accurate prediction of joint kinematics from wearable inertial sensors in subject-independent settings
[
9
]
. Transfer learning strategies have been proposed to improve personalization when limited subject-specific data are available
[
11
]
.
Recent works have extended IMU-based learning to the estimation of joint moments and ground reaction forces across multiple locomotion conditions
[
10
,
21
]
. These approaches show promising cross-subject generalization in offline evaluations. However, adaptation to new users is typically handled via post hoc fine-tuning or complete subject-specific retraining approaches that generally rely on non-trivial amounts of subject-specific data. Systematic investigation of structured, rapid initial adaptation strategies, compatible with real-world deployment constraints, remains limited.
Adaptive control strategies have been widely adopted in wearable robotics to achieve subject-specific assistance through impedance modulation and interaction-force feedback within assist-as-needed paradigms
[
24
]
. Extensions toward dynamic synchronization and task-dependent trajectory adaptation have been proposed using adaptive movement primitives and interaction-aware controllers
[
29
]
. Human-in-the-loop control frameworks further integrate stability constraints and ergonomic objectives to improve robustness across different environmental conditions
[
16
]
.
Data-driven personalization has increasingly been explored to reduce reliance on explicit human–robot modeling. Reinforcement learning has been applied to optimize assistive torque profiles and timing parameters
[
31
]
, while Bayesian optimization and neural trajectory translators have been incorporated for multi-task generalization
[
4
]
. Although effective, these approaches typically require iterative interaction cycles or extended adaptation phases.
To reduce calibration effort, meta-learning and few-shot transfer strategies have recently emerged. Model-agnostic meta-learning (MAML) has been used to personalize myoelectric upper-limb exoskeleton control with short calibration sequences
[
26
]
. Similarly, Reptile-based few-shot transfer learning has been investigated for wearable IMU-based human activity recognition (HAR) under limited labeled data
[
7
]
, and broader surveys highlight generalization challenges in IMU-based HAR
[
2
]
. Despite these advances, existing methods predominantly focus on trajectory generation, torque tuning, motion classification, or activity recognition, rather than continuous neuromuscular regression under strict data-efficiency and deployment constraints.
III
Methodology
The overall method is summarized in Fig.
1
, and it is composed of three main blocks: Signal Processing, GEGLU-Transformer, and Few-Shot Adaptation. The methodology was evaluated on a gait dataset
[
3
]
, enabling direct comparison with existing approaches in the locomotion literature. Although the experimental validation focuses on gait data, the problem formulation and the proposed architecture are task-agnostic. The framework operates on synchronized kinematic and myoelectric time series and does not embed gait-specific assumptions. Therefore, it can be directly extended to upper-limb movements or other robotic and assistive scenarios.
Figure 1:
Overall pipeline. Top: signal processing workflow including temporal alignment, movement segmentation, filtering, and normalization of IMU and EMG signals. Bottom: GEGLU-Transformer architecture with temporal convolutional embedding, positional encoding, four self-attention encoder layers, and final linear projection for multi-muscle EMG prediction. A lightweight few-shot adaptation module enables subject-specific calibration at deployment via limited gradient-based updates.
Figure 2:
Left: Self-attention encoder block with multi-head attention and residual connections. Right: GEGLU-based feed-forward module.

III-B
Signal Processing
Before being used for model training, raw EMG and IMU signals need to be pre-processed. Therefore, we used the same pipeline described in
[
13
]
, ensuring methodological consistency with prior IMU-to-EMG estimation frameworks and standard electrophysiological practices.
EMG Processing
Surface EMG signals are processed according to the recommendations of the International Society of Electrophysiology and Kinesiology (ISEK) standards
[
20
]
. Signals are sampled at 1000 Hz and band-pass filtered using a fourth-order Butterworth filter (20–450 Hz) to attenuate motion artifacts and high-frequency noise. The filtered signals are then full-wave rectified and subsequently low-pass filtered at 8 Hz using a fourth-order Butterworth filter to extract smooth muscle activation envelopes suitable for regression modeling.
Temporal Alignment and Resampling
IMU signals are sampled at 200 Hz. EMG envelopes are temporally aligned with IMU signals and resampled to 200 Hz via linear interpolation to ensure synchronized input–target pairs.
Movement Segmentation
The dataset is segmented into a set of stand-alone movement sequences. In the used walking dataset, heel-strike events are employed to segment the data into individual gait cycles. Each cycle is time-normalized to 101 samples representing 0–100% of the gait cycle. This normalization enables consistent temporal modeling across subjects and walking conditions.
Post-Segmentation Filtering
After time normalization, segmented IMU and EMG signals were median-filtered to attenuate residual impulsive artifacts and independently scaled to
[
0
,
1
]
[0,1]
via min–max normalization.
III-C
Gaussian Error Gated Linear Unit Transformer
The proposed architecture builds upon the Transformer encoder framework originally introduced in
[
32
]
. Compared to recurrent models, Transformers enable parallel sequence processing while preserving global temporal context through multi-head attention. An overview of the complete processing and modeling pipeline is illustrated in Fig.
1
, where the bottom panel presents the proposed GEGLU-Transformer architecture, composed of a temporal convolutional embedding layer, positional encoding, four stacked self-attention encoder layers, and a final linear projection head for multi-muscle EMG prediction.
Each encoder layer follows the standard Transformer structure with residual connections and layer normalization, as shown in Fig.
2
. The self-attention block enables the model to capture long-range temporal dependencies across multimodal sensor inputs.
Convolutional Front-End
Given the high temporal resolution and multi-channel structure of IMU signals, a 1D convolutional front-end is first applied to extract local motion patterns prior to attention-based modeling. Convolutional preprocessing has been shown to improve stability and inductive bias in time-series Transformers.
Specifically, a Conv1D layer (kernel size 5) projects the 24 inertial features into a 256-dimensional latent space. Group Normalization is employed to enhance robustness under moderate batch sizes, followed by Gaussian Error Linear Units (GELU) activations, which have become standard in Transformer-based architectures.
Self-Attention Encoder
Temporal dependencies are modeled through a stack of four Transformer encoder layers with multi-head self-attention (8 heads). A Pre-Norm configuration is adopted, where Layer Normalization is applied before both the attention and feed-forward blocks, improving optimization stability in deep Transformers. Dropout regularization is applied after both the attention and feed-forward sublayers to improve generalization.
GEGLU Feed-Forward
Instead of the standard position-wise feed-forward network, we employ a Gaussian Error Gated Linear Unit (GEGLU) formulation
[
27
]
. Gating mechanisms improve feature selection and gradient flow by modulating channel-wise activations as reported in Fig.
2
. Formally, given input
x
x
, the feed-forward transformation is defined as:

\mathrm{FF}_{\text{GEGLU}}(x)=W_{o}\big(\mathrm{GELU}(W_{1}x)\odot(W_{2}x)\big),
(3)
where
GELU
​
(
⋅
)
\mathrm{GELU}(\cdot)
denotes the Gaussian Error Linear Unit activation and
⊙
\odot
element-wise multiplication.
Compared to sigmoid-based gating, GEGLU provides smoother activation dynamics and improved gradient propagation, which is particularly beneficial for neuromuscular signal regression.
Positional Encoding
Since self-attention is permutation-invariant, fixed positional embeddings are added to preserve temporal ordering. The positional encoding is added to the latent sequence prior to the Transformer stack.
Output Projection
After the encoder stack, Layer Normalization is applied before a linear projection, mapping the 256-dimensional latent representation to the target EMG dimensionality.
The model performs sequence-to-sequence regression, predicting continuous EMG envelopes over the normalized movement segment.
III-D
Few-shot Adaptation
Given a small adaptation set
𝒟
ada
\mathcal{D}_{\text{ada}}
composed of movement segments from an unseen test subject, the model parameters are adapted by minimizing the mean squared error (MSE) loss defined in Eq. (
2
). All parameters are updated (i.e., no layer freezing is applied), enabling full-network personalization to subject-specific neuromuscular patterns. Adaptation is performed under strict data and computational constraints to mitigate catastrophic forgetting and ensure numerical stability.
IV
Experimental Evaluation
IV-A
Dataset
The proposed framework was evaluated using the publicly available lower-limb biomechanics dataset
[
3
]
. The dataset comprises synchronized inertial measurements and surface electromyography recordings collected from 22 healthy adults during multiple locomotion tasks. Each participant was instrumented with four wearable IMUs placed on the lower limbs, providing tri-axial accelerometer and gyroscope signals sampled at 200 Hz. Surface EMG was recorded from eleven lower-limb muscles at 1000 Hz using bipolar electrodes. Out of the eleven recorded muscles, ten lower-limb muscles were considered in this work:
gastrocnemius medialis
,
tibialis anterior
,
soleus
,
vastus medialis
,
vastus lateralis
,
rectus femoris
,
biceps femoris
,
semitendinosus
,
gracilis
, and
gluteus medius
. The
right external oblique
was excluded from the analysis, as it is not directly involved in lower-limb actuation and was outside the scope of the proposed neuromuscular modeling framework.
The dataset includes treadmill walking at multiple speeds, level-ground walking, stair ascent/descent, and ramp ascent/descent. For this study, only IMU signals were used as model inputs, while processed EMG envelopes were used as ground-truth neuromuscular targets.
IV-B
Experimental Protocol
A leave-one-subject-out (LOSO) cross-validation scheme was adopted to evaluate the proposed method. For each fold, one subject was held out for testing, while the remaining subjects were used for training. The base model was trained offline using only training-subject data. Model selection and hyperparameter tuning were performed exclusively on a validation subset extracted from the training pool, ensuring strict subject independence and preventing data leakage. To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed approach, we compared with two state-of-the-art models that have already being used for IMU-to-EMG estimation in walking task. We first reproduced the LSTM-based estimator proposed in
[
13
]
, followed by its CNN-LSTM extension introduced in
[
12
]
. The CNN-LSTM architecture was further refined by replacing the original 2D convolutional processing (which treated the input as a time–feature image) with 1D temporal convolutions, preserving the sequential structure of inertial signals prior to recurrent modeling. Batch normalization and deeper LSTM layers with dropout were introduced to improve optimization stability and temporal representation capacity.
In their original works, both the baseline methods considered only level-ground walking conditions, excluding the other walking tasks. Thus, an initial benchmarking phase was conducted using level-ground walking data only, for both training and testing. This stage allows a fair comparison with prior IMU-to-EMG approaches and isolates architectural differences without the confounding effect of multiple locomotion modes. After completing the level-ground benchmarking phase, all models were retrained from scratch using the full multi-condition dataset, including level-ground, stair ascent/descent, ramp walking, and treadmill locomotion.
IV-C
Training
The proposed model, as well as the state-of-the-art baseline models, were optimized using stochastic gradient-based methods. The LSTM and CNN–LSTM architectures were trained with the Adam optimizer using a learning rate of
10
−
3
10^{-3}
, whereas the GEGLU-Transformer model employed AdamW with a learning rate of
3
×
10
−
4
3\times 10^{-4}
and weight decay of
10
−
2
10^{-2}
to improve regularization of the attention-based architecture. MSE was adopted as the regression objective for all models. Within each LOSO fold, data from the training subjects were randomly partitioned into 80% training and 20% validation subsets. The held-out test subject was strictly excluded from all stages of offline optimization, including model selection and hyperparameter tuning. Models were trained for a maximum of 1000 epochs using mini-batches of size 128. Early stopping with a patience of 50 epochs was applied based on validation loss, and the model achieving the lowest validation error was retained for deployment. All experiments were implemented in PyTorch and executed on a GPU-enabled high-performance computing cluster equipped with NVIDIA RTX GPUs.
IV-D
Deployment
At deployment, the trained models were evaluated on unseen subjects. As explained in Section III-D, a few-shot adaptation strategy was applied, where a small fraction of subject-specific gait cycles was used for initial calibration. Calibration ratios of
0.5
%
0.5\%
,
1
%
1\%
,
2
%
2\%
,
5
%
5\%
, and
10
%
10\%
of the test set were evaluated under the LOSO protocol. Across folds, these percentages correspond on average to approximately
10
10

22

46

241

∼

test cycles per subject. During subject-specific calibration, model parameters were updated using a reduced learning rate of
5
×
10
−
5
5\times 10^{-5}
. Adam was used for the CNN and CNN–LSTM models, while AdamW was adopted for the GEGLU-Transformer architecture to maintain consistency with its offline optimization scheme. For each calibration phase, 40 gradient update steps were performed over the selected subject-specific gait cycles. All network parameters were updated (no layer freezing), and gradient norm clipping with threshold 1.0 was applied to enhance numerical stability during adaptation.
IV-E
Metrics
Model performance was evaluated using complementary accuracy and physiological consistency metrics. Standard regression measures included normalized root mean square error (nRMSE), Pearson correlation coefficient (
r
r
), and coefficient of determination (
R

), assessing overall reconstruction fidelity and temporal similarity between predicted and ground-truth EMG envelopes.

V
Results
This section evaluates the proposed GEGLU-Transformer under cross-subject generalization and few-shot adaptation, analyzing performance at global, muscle-specific, and locomotion-specific levels.
V-A
Impact of GEGLU Gating
We evaluated the impact of replacing the standard feed-forward block with a GEGLU-gated formulation. Although improvements are moderate in absolute terms, they are consistent across metrics. GEGLU yields a relative gain of
+
0.33
%
+0.33\%
in correlation (
r
r
),
+
1.52
%
+1.52\%
in
R
2
R^{2}
, and a
−
0.65
%
-0.65\%
reduction in nRMSE compared to the non-gated Transformer. Peak-related metrics, however, do not improve and remain comparable to the non-gated variants.
Importantly, performance variability is slightly reduced, indicating improved cross-subject stability. Given the negligible increase in architectural complexity and the systematic improvement across evaluation metrics, we adopt GEGLU in the final model as a more robust feed-forward formulation.
V-B

reports the cross-subject performance under the LOSO protocol. Overall, GEGLU-Transformer demonstrates superior sample efficiency across locomotion conditions, particularly in more dynamic tasks.
VI
Discussion
The experimental results highlight three main findings. First, the proposed GEGLU-Transformer consistently improves cross-subject generalization compared to the baselines, both when considering all locomotion modalities jointly and when restricting the analysis to level-ground only. This indicates that the performance gain is not condition-specific but reflects a more robust subject-invariant representation. This suggests that self-attention mechanisms are more effective in modeling long-range temporal dependencies in neuromuscular signals, while gated feed-forward modulation enhances feature selectivity and stability. Second, the advantage becomes particularly evident in amplitude-related metrics. The reduction in peak estimation errors indicates that the model better captures activation magnitude, not only temporal alignment. This is especially relevant for applications involving assistive control or torque estimation, where amplitude fidelity directly impacts actuation accuracy. Third, the online adaptation analysis reveals a markedly faster personalization dynamic. The GEGLU-Transformer reaches near-plateau performance with minimal calibration data, indicating that the learned representation is well-structured and easily fine-tuned. In practical robotic settings, this reduces calibration time and increases usability in real-world deployments. Performance differences across muscles further suggest that high-amplitude and more regularly patterned muscles (e.g., plantarflexors and vasti) benefit most from the proposed architecture, whereas biarticular or low-activation muscles remain challenging for all models. This indicates that part of the prediction difficulty may stem from signal quality and biomechanical variability rather than model capacity alone. These findings suggest that self-attention with gated feed-forward modulation enables stronger global modeling and more efficient adaptation than recurrent approaches.
VII
Conclusion
This work introduces a GEGLU-Transformer architecture for cross-subject neuromuscular signal reconstruction and few-shot adaptation. Under the LOSO protocol, the proposed model consistently outperformed recurrent baselines across all evaluation metrics, demonstrating improved waveform similarity, reduced amplitude and timing errors, and stronger generalization to unseen subjects. Notably, these improvements were observed both across mixed locomotion modalities and under level-ground-only evaluation, confirming condition-independent robustness. In addition, the GEGLU-Transformer exhibited significantly faster adaptation. Substantial performance gains were achieved with as little adaptation data, and performance rapidly approached a plateau, indicating efficient subject-specific personalization. This behavior suggests that the proposed adaptation framework is methodologically general and can extend beyond lower-limb locomotion to other human movements, including upper-limb tasks.
However, the dataset includes fixed stair geometries and incline levels, potentially limiting generalization to different environmental configurations. Future work will investigate robustness to varying terrain geometries and continuous adaptation in dynamically changing real-world environments.

preview_text:
Reliable estimation of neuromuscular activation is a key enabler for adaptive and personalized control in wearable robotics. However, surface electromyography (EMG) remains difficult to deploy robustly outside laboratory settings due to electrode sensitivity, signal non-stationarity, and strong subject dependence. In this work, we propose an adaptive IMU-to-EMG learning framework that reconstructs continuous muscle activation envelopes from wearable inertial measurements across heterogeneous movement conditions. The approach combines a Transformer encoder with Gaussian Error Gated Linear Units (GEGLU-Transformer) to enhance cross-subject generalization and enable rapid subject-specific personalization. Under a strict leave-one-subject-out (LOSO) protocol on a multi-condition lower-limb biomechanics dataset, the proposed architecture achieves r = 0.706 +/- 0.139 and R^2 = 0.474 +/- 0.208 without subject-specific adaptation. With only 0.5% adaptation data, performance increases to r = 0.761 +/- 0.030 and R^2 = 0.559 +/- 0.047, demonstrating rapid adaptation and early performance saturation. These results support attention-based architectures combined with lightweight adaptation as a practical and scalable alternative to direct EMG sensing for real-world wearable robotic applications.

GEGLU-Transformer for IMU-to-EMG Estimation with Few-Shot Adaptation

Emanuele Menegatti,
4

All authors are with the Department of Information Engineering, University of Padua, 35131 Padua, Italy.
Corresponding author: Miroljub Mihailovic (e-mail: miroljub.mihailovic@phd.unipd.it).

Reliable estimation of neuromuscular activation is a key enabler for adaptive and personalized control in wearable robotics. However, surface electromyography (EMG) remains difficult to deploy robustly outside laboratory settings due to electrode sensitivity, signal non-stationarity, and strong subject dependence.
